draft.introduction: Jonah 1 opens with the prophet’s flight from God’s command to preach to Nineveh, Israel’s enemy. The narrative subverts prophetic convention: the pagan sailors prove more pious than the prophet himself.

translation.text: Now the word of the Lord came to Jonah son of Amittai, saying, ‘Arise, go to Nineveh, that great city, and call out against it.’ But Jonah rose to flee to Tarshish from the presence of the Lord. So they picked up Jonah and hurled him into the sea, and the sea ceased from its raging. And the Lord appointed a great fish to swallow up Jonah.
translation.original_text: καὶ ἐγένετο λόγος Κυρίου πρὸς Ιωνᾶν τὸν τοῦ Αμαθι

translator_notes.note: Jonah’s flight “from the presence of the Lord” is theologically absurd—as Psalm 139 makes clear, there is nowhere to flee from God. The narrative uses this absurdity deliberately, exposing the prophet’s nationalism: he would rather die than see Israel’s enemies receive mercy.
